meta.title: What's happening
meta.note: Curated cross-domain event log — the developments moving Mexico, each rewritten in plain English, dated, and linked to a first-party or record source. Auto-generated from the news wire on every refresh; raw or non-English items are quarantined, never published.

events.title: Former governor Ruffo's fuel-smuggling hearing drags on with army links alleged
events.why: The ongoing case against ex-Baja California governor Ruffo includes a businessman accused of running a separate fuel-contraband network involving Defense Ministry personnel, tying the trial to broader concerns about military involvement in huachicol fiscal.
events.background: Huachicol fiscal is the tax-fraud scheme of smuggling fuel while declaring only part of it, and Ruffo was the first opposition politician to unseat the PRI as Baja California governor.
events.drivers: The case also implicates businessman Riestra Fernández, accused of running a separate contraband network involving Defense Ministry personnel, while political rhetoric for and against Ruffo's detention keeps building.
events.implications: The article notes the case matters to both sides: PAN feels bound to defend a historic figure, while Morena, facing its own governors accused of organized-crime ties, gets a chance to shift attention.

events.title: Mexico files criminal complaints against ICE agents over migrant deaths
events.why: Mexico's government is testing a criminal law route after 17 Mexicans died in ICE custody, raising the diplomatic temperature with Washington over immigration enforcement.
events.background: ICE detention centers are privately run facilities that hold migrants for US federal enforcement operations, a system that has expanded sharply under the current administration.
events.drivers: An Associated Press count puts deaths tied to the enforcement drive at 62, including 17 Mexicans, after agents shot two people and a Venezuelan detainee died of a heart attack during a transfer in Georgia.
events.implications: The complaints raise the diplomatic tone between Mexico and Washington over how migrants are treated in US custody.

events.title: Mexico says 83% of gas stations now comply with diesel price cap
events.why: The federal government's voluntary ceiling of 27 pesos per liter for diesel has been adopted by most stations, a step meant to contain fuel costs and inflation pressure.
events.background: Mexico's diesel price cap is a voluntary pact among fuel retailers, coordinated with Energy, Finance, Pemex and the consumer agency Profeco, meant to hold pump prices near 27 pesos per liter.
events.implications: Diesel feeds directly into freight and logistics costs, so uneven compliance means transport-cost relief varies sharply by state rather than applying nationwide.

events.title: Sheinbaum faces scrutiny over Morena governors' alleged cartel ties
events.why: Reports point to governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora facing US scrutiny over suspected links to organized crime, testing the government's response as it downplays the allegations.
events.background: Sheinbaum has built her US relationship on a pragmatic, low-friction approach even as Mexico's federal judiciary and states operate under new electoral rules following the 2024 judicial reform.
events.drivers: Leaked audio and reports have widened US scrutiny to governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora, with Baja California's Marina del Pilar Ávila the latest named.
events.implications: The escalating US allegations test the sovereignty-based defense Sheinbaum has used for four months, even as she has managed the Trump relationship carefully.

events.title: Former Baja California governor Ruffo detained in cartel-linked case
events.why: The arrest of the first opposition governor in recent memory lands amid US scrutiny of Morena governors' alleged cartel ties, reshaping the political fight over organized crime.
events.background: Ernesto Ruffo, Mexico's first opposition governor after defeating the PRI in Baja California in 1989, is now central to a fuel-smuggling probe the government says has run over a year.
events.drivers: Ruffo's arrest gives Morena political relief during one of its most difficult weeks handling the Baja California and Sinaloa cartel-tie scandals.
events.implications: The case reframes the corruption narrative toward opposition figures rather than Morena governors, and marks the first time in Sheinbaum's term an opposition figure has been detained.

events.title: Chinese-made heavy trucks in Mexico complicate bid to ease Trump tariffs
events.why: A growing fleet of China-built trucks operating in Mexico gives Washington another argument to maintain tariff pressure, tying trade relief to scrutiny of Chinese content in Mexico's supply chains.
events.background: USMCA's annual review process now scrutinizes whether Mexico's export platform, especially trucking, is becoming a route for Chinese manufacturers into North America.
events.drivers: Chinese truck import value into Mexico rose more than sevenfold over six years and the number of Chinese truck firms operating there reached about 23, alarming US officials and Mexican manufacturers.
events.implications: The trend complicates Mexican negotiators' push for tariff relief on an integrated export industry, since it undercuts Mexico's case for alignment with the US against China.

events.title: Large taxpayers account for nearly half of Mexico's tax revenue
events.why: Corporations with annual revenue above 1,500 million pesos form a small group that funds a large share of public finances, underscoring Mexico's reliance on a concentrated tax base.
events.background: Mexico's tax system runs through SAT, whose Administración General de Grandes Contribuyentes oversees corporations earning over 1,500 million pesos a year, financial entities and state productive companies.
events.drivers: A tiny share of the taxpayer roll, about 0.02%, generates nearly half of federal tax revenue, making this segment central to public finances.
events.implications: The SAT had covered only 29.14% of its import-tax collection goal by the end of May, showing the strain on broadening revenue beyond this small group.

events.title: Supreme Court justices' salaries cut for second consecutive year
events.why: The reduction in pay and benefits for Mexico's top judges follows a broader push to trim public-sector compensation, extending austerity measures into the judiciary.
events.background: The 2024 judicial reform replaced Supreme Court appointment with election, and pay for the new justices is constitutionally capped so no official earns more than the president.
events.drivers: Official pay tables in the 2026 federal budget cut justices' compensation roughly 35% from the previous court, which had earned about 206,000 pesos net monthly plus benefits.
events.implications: Even after the cut, to up to 191,657 pesos, Supreme Court salaries remain among the highest in the federal public sector.

events.title: Mexican health authorities investigate cyclosporiasis outbreak tied to Taylor Farms
events.why: Cofepris and Senasica are investigating after US authorities reported 1,644 cases linked to the supplier, prompting preventive measures in Mexico amid cross-border food safety concerns.
events.background: Cofepris and Senasica are Mexico's health and agricultural safety regulators, tasked with inspecting food exporters and responding to cross-border outbreaks.
events.drivers: US authorities reported 1,644 cyclosporiasis cases tied to shredded iceberg lettuce, prompting Taylor Farms to recall products sourced from Mexico.
events.implications: Mexican authorities describe their response as preventive only, with inspections and traceability analysis but no confirmed cause yet.
events.next: A joint technical group has been formed to investigate and adopt further preventive measures.

events.title: Six journalists killed in Mexico since January amid press attacks
events.why: Ongoing attacks on media outlets and reporters across the country have left six journalists dead this year, with arrests made in only one case, raising questions about the state's protection of the press.
events.background: Mexico's press operates amid recurring violence against reporters, with INEGI data showing most crimes nationally go unreported or uninvestigated.
events.drivers: Six journalists have been killed since January, the latest Josué Martínez Contreras, shot in Puebla while his 13-year-old son was present.
events.implications: Arrests have been made in only one of the six killings, underscoring gaps in protection and prosecution.

events.title: Mexico cuts World Cup economic impact forecast
events.why: Deloitte lowered its estimate of the tournament's economic impact on Mexico by 7%, citing weaker than expected performance in restaurants, hotels and entertainment venues.
events.background: The 2026 World Cup spans the US, Mexico and Canada with 48 teams and 104 matches, and Mexico had hoped the tournament would deliver a large economic boost through tourism and spending.
events.drivers: Deloitte cut its estimate by 7% after weaker than expected performance in restaurants, hotels and entertainment venues.

events.title: Mexico returns letters to ICE, testing Sheinbaum's approach to Washington
events.why: The Trump administration accuses Mexico of overstepping its sovereignty by protesting migrant deaths in detention centers, a dispute that tests how far Sheinbaum will push diplomatic channels versus public confrontation.
events.background: Mexico's protest letters over migrant deaths in ICE custody were sent through its embassy, the normal diplomatic channel with Washington.
events.drivers: The US State Department, through official Michael Kozak, returned the letters to Mexico's new ambassador Roberto Lazzeri and told Mexico to use diplomatic channels rather than public protest.
events.implications: The episode tests whether Sheinbaum's government can keep pressing the ICE deaths issue without straining the channel Washington insists on using.

events.title: Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo arrested on organized crime charges
events.why: Prosecutors said the arrest, tied to a fuel-theft investigation into his company, followed a high-complexity probe; a business partner was also detained.
events.background: Huachicol fiscal, or fuel-tax smuggling, is a recurring target of Mexican prosecutors investigating customs and tax fraud tied to fuel imports.
events.drivers: The FGR describes a high-complexity investigation into Ingemar, a company Ruffo founded that he has said only handled customs paperwork, leading to his arrest along with business partner Ricardo Thompson Navarro.
events.implications: A former governor now faces organized crime and tax-fuel-smuggling charges, tying a prominent political figure to an alleged contraband network.
events.next: Ruffo has been transferred, handcuffed, to FGR facilities in Tijuana.

events.title: Honda halts sales of its Mexico-made electric SUV in the US
events.why: Honda will pull the Prologue EV from the US market and shift toward hybrids, citing weak electric vehicle demand, a setback for Mexico's EV manufacturing push.
events.background: The Honda Prologue is an electric SUV built in Mexico and co-developed with General Motors, part of Mexico's push to attract EV manufacturing investment.
events.drivers: Honda cited weak US demand for the decision, telling dealers production will end this year as the company shifts toward gasoline hybrids and combustion models.
events.implications: The move follows other automakers scaling back EV production in response to soft demand in the US market.
events.next: Sales will stop starting with the 2026 model year as Honda ends Prologue production.

events.title: Pemex halts oil exports to Cuba for first time in 33 years
events.why: The state oil company stops shipments after Trump administration warnings, a move that signals Mexico's caution in handling energy ties with Havana under US pressure.
events.background: Pemex is Mexico's state oil company, financially tied to the federal government, and Cuba has long received Mexican crude as one of its main fuel sources.
events.drivers: Exports fell to zero dollars in May, a decline that began in January amid US pressure on Mexico to avoid supporting Havana, with President Trump threatening intervention against Díaz-Canel.
events.next: Despite Trump's threats, Sheinbaum said in late June that Mexico will keep sending petroleum to Cuba.
events.implications: Exports to Cuba fell to zero in May, the first such stoppage since records began in 1993, showing Mexico's caution in balancing US pressure against its own foreign-policy stance.

events.title: Slim's Grupo Carso buys 30% stake in Mexican oil field from TotalEnergies
events.why: Carso will take a stake in Block 30, home to the KAN field operated by Harbour Energy, extending the conglomerate's push into Mexico's oil sector as the government seeks private investment in energy.
events.background: Pemex's crude output has fallen to its lowest level in four decades, pushing the state company to seek private partners like Grupo Carso, which already works as a Pemex service contractor on fields such as Ixachi.
events.drivers: Carso is buying TotalEnergies' 30% stake in Block 30, home to the KAN field, alongside operator Harbour Energy, which holds the remaining 70%.
events.next: The deal's closing depends on obtaining the corresponding government authorizations.
events.implications: Slim has said Pemex's low production is Mexico's core problem, even after declining in May to pursue further business directly with the state oil company.

events.title: Mexico issues travel advisory for the US over intestinal infection outbreak
events.why: The advisory responds to a rise in cyclosporiasis cases across several US states, an unusual reversal of the typical direction of health warnings between the two countries.
events.background: Sinave is Mexico's national epidemiological surveillance system, which typically issues warnings about outbreaks abroad affecting Mexican travelers.
events.implications: The advisory marks an unusual reversal, with Mexico now cautioning its citizens about conditions in the United States rather than the other way around.
events.drivers: Cyclosporiasis cases have risen in Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio and West Virginia, with more than 400 cases accumulated in the outbreak.

events.title: BlackRock says annual USMCA reviews will bring uncertainty to Mexico investment
events.why: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose its sovereign investment-grade rating, but flags the treaty's annual review mechanism as a recurring source of uncertainty for investors in the country.
events.background: USMCA's annual review mechanism, triggered after the US declined a 16-year extension, has replaced the certainty of a long guaranteed term with yearly reassessment through 2036.
events.drivers: BlackRock's José Luis Ortega said the US refusal to renew removed the chance for greater investment certainty this year, leaving long-term capital unwilling to commit aggressively.
events.implications: BlackRock does not expect Mexico to lose investment-grade status, but says persistent uncertainty means high economic growth from investment is unlikely to materialize.

events.title: Finance ministry accelerates cuts to productive investment to control fiscal deficit
events.why: Hacienda is cutting spending on productive investment ahead of the second half of the year in an effort to contain the fiscal deficit, according to the report.
events.background: Hacienda, Mexico's finance ministry, is trying to narrow the fiscal deficit partly by leaning on Pemex, the state oil company that already strains public finances through the support it requires.
events.drivers: Despite SAT collecting more than ever from tax credits this year, physical investment in Pemex fell to 77,188 million pesos in the first five months, its lowest level since 2008.
events.implications: Capital for new Pemex machinery fell 45.7% in real terms over five months, its steepest historical drop for the period, even as social development spending continues rising.

events.title: Regulator CNBV fines BanFeliz amid possible change of control
events.why: The banking regulator issued four fines of 481,100 pesos each against the digital bank, its first sanctions, as the institution appears to be undergoing an ownership change.
events.background: The CNBV is Mexico's banking regulator; BanFeliz took control of Banco Forjadores in 2024, inheriting an institution that had already accumulated regulatory sanctions.
events.drivers: The fines stem from monitoring, procedural and client-verification deficiencies dated to 2022, before BanFeliz's takeover, and mark the first sanctions published under its administration.
events.implications: None of the infractions were classified as serious, and all remain open to challenge, which limits their weight amid the reported change of control.
events.next: The sanctions are subject to appeal by the bank.

events.title: Mexico's imports from Asia grow 42% despite tariffs
events.why: Purchases from Taiwan, China and other Asian countries rose 42.4% through May while trade with the Americas grew only 4.5%, suggesting tariff pressure has not curbed Asian sourcing.
events.background: Mexico applies tariffs on 11 countries without a trade agreement, including China, India, South Korea, Vietnam, Thailand and Taiwan.
events.drivers: Imports from Asia rose from $108,971 million to $155,226 million between January and May, led by a 233.76% jump in purchases from Taiwan, while imports from the Americas grew only 4.49%.
events.implications: Asia's share of Mexico's total imports climbed to 50%, suggesting the tariffs have not curbed the shift toward Asian sourcing.

events.title: Toyota's Tacoma displaces Mexico's top vehicle export to the US
events.why: The Tacoma became the single most-exported vehicle from Mexico to the United States in the first half of 2026, according to trade data cited in the report.
events.background: The Tacoma is built at Toyota's Tijuana plant (TMMBC) and has been Mexico's top vehicle export to the United States; Mexico exported 1.28 million vehicles to the US in the first half of 2026, down 3.6% annually.
events.drivers: Toyota is moving Tacoma production to its San Antonio, Texas plant, a shift it says will be gradual over about four years.
events.implications: Mexico stands to lose its leading vehicle-export line, since Tacoma and Tacoma HEV units made up 124,871 and 15,665 of Toyota's 140,536 exports in the period.
events.next: Toyota expects the San Antonio plant's output to grow by about 150,000 units, close to the volume currently exported from Mexico, as the transfer proceeds over roughly four years.

events.title: Mexico's M&A deal value more than doubles in first half of 2026
events.why: Mergers and acquisitions reached 10,458 million dollars in the first six months of the year, led by deals involving Fibra Next and Sierra Madre Gold, pointing to renewed corporate dealmaking despite trade uncertainty.
events.background: Mexican M&A activity spans real estate, industrial and mining sectors, with Fibra Next and Sierra Madre Gold among the period's larger deals.
events.drivers: Investors concentrated capital in larger real estate, industrial and mining deals, including Fibra Next's purchase of the Atlas Portfolio and Sierra Madre Gold and Silver's acquisition of the Del Toro mine.
events.implications: Deal count fell 23.3% even as total value more than doubled, which Seale & Associates partner Carlos Hernández described as a recomposition toward larger transactions rather than a market slowdown.

events.title: Foxconn and Invetec deepen Taiwan's tech investment in Mexico
events.why: Taiwanese manufacturers including Foxconn and Invetec are expanding investment in Mexico, much of it tied to AI hardware, reflecting the growing trade and technology relationship between the two economies.
events.background: Taiwanese manufacturers have expanded in Mexico as AI hardware demand grows, building on an already deepening trade relationship between the two economies.
events.implications: The expansions position Mexico more deeply in AI hardware supply chains and point to further Taiwanese investment interest.
events.next: Other Taiwanese companies are reported to be considering investments in the country.
events.drivers: Inventec is investing $450 million to expand its Ciudad Juárez data-center design operations, while Foxconn has committed $136.7 million this year for AI server production, on top of $168 million announced in August 2025.

events.title: Grupo Carso buys TotalEnergies' 30% stake in a Gulf of Mexico oil block
events.why: Carlos Slim's conglomerate expands into offshore oil exploration, adding private capital to a sector still dominated by Pemex.
events.background: Grupo Carso is Carlos Slim's conglomerate, which has been expanding into Gulf of Mexico oil and gas blocks as Pemex, facing declining crude output, seeks private partners.
events.drivers: Carso is buying TotalEnergies' 30% stake in Block 30 through a subsidiary, with Harbour Energy retaining 70% and continuing as operator.
events.next: The deal's closing is subject to regulatory approval.
events.implications: Slim has said his companies will avoid new joint ventures with Pemex, so the purchase extends his push into oil and gas segments outside state control rather than partnering with the state company.

events.title: Banxico deputy governor says inflation risks remain tilted to the upside
events.why: Jonathan Heath said the balance of risks has not turned favorable despite June's slowdown, a signal the central bank may hold off on further rate cuts.
events.background: Banxico targets 3% inflation, give or take one percentage point, and Jonathan Heath sits on its governing board that sets the policy rate.
events.implications: His comment that the balance of risks is not yet favorable suggests Banxico may proceed cautiously on further rate moves.
events.drivers: Heath noted June's inflation reading, at 3.37% annually, understated how favorable the second half of the month actually was, but he questioned whether the improvement will last through the rest of the year.
events.next: Heath said the balance of risks is not yet favorable, signaling continued close monitoring of incoming data before any shift in stance.

events.title: Honda to end electric vehicle production in Coahuila
events.why: Weak EV demand is prompting Honda to halt its electric model in Mexico, following a similar pullback by Toyota and raising questions about EV manufacturing plans in the country's auto export belt.
events.background: Honda's Prologue is an electric SUV developed with General Motors and built at the Ramos Arizpe plant in Coahuila; it launched for the 2026 model year.
events.implications: The decision raises questions about the future of electric-vehicle manufacturing plans at Mexican plants built for the US market.
events.next: Production at Ramos Arizpe will stop by the end of the year, ending Prologue sales with the 2026 model.
events.drivers: Weak US demand for electric vehicles is pushing Honda to end Prologue sales after the 2026 model, shifting its US strategy toward hybrids and combustion-engine vehicles that show more market activity.

events.title: Sheinbaum says courts will be impartial in fuel-theft case against ex-governor Ruffo
events.why: The president rejected claims of political persecution, saying the investigation into former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo began a year ago and more arrests are expected.
events.background: Huachicol fiscal is fuel-tax contraband, and Sheinbaum's daily press conference is where the government routinely responds to political controversies like Ruffo's arrest.
events.drivers: Sheinbaum said the investigation had been running for a year before eight arrest warrants were issued, rejecting the idea that it targets the opposition politically.
events.implications: The case tests whether Mexico's newly elected judiciary can rule independently in a politically charged prosecution involving the opposition.
events.next: Security chief García Harfuch said more arrest warrants remain to be issued and additional people are under investigation.

events.title: Officials detail huachicol network linked to former governor Ruffo
events.why: Security chief García Harfuch said the fuel-theft ring declared only 10% of its rail tanker capacity, part of an investigation open more than a year that has produced eight arrests so far.
events.background: The huachicol fiscal case against Ruffo stems from a fuel-contraband network investigated by the Fiscalía Especializada en Materia de Delincuencia Organizada.
events.drivers: Investigators traced the network after seizing 33 rail tankers and found the organization declared only about 10% of each tanker's real capacity to evade fiscal controls.
events.implications: Authorities consider it among the largest fuel-contraband structures they have identified.
events.next: The investigation, open more than a year, continues.

events.title: US-Iran conflict threatens a fifth of global oil supply, pressuring gasoline prices
events.why: Shippers are avoiding the Strait of Hormuz as the standoff escalates, raising logistics costs that could feed through to fuel prices in Mexico.
events.background: Mexico imports large volumes of US natural gas for its power system, tying its electricity supply to developments in global energy markets.
events.implications: The added shipping costs threaten to feed through to fuel prices just as international markets were beginning to stabilize after an earlier round of increases.
events.drivers: Renewed US-Iran conflict is forcing oil companies and shipping operators to avoid the strait, adding up to 14 days and higher costs by rerouting around Africa's Cape of Good Hope.

events.title: US health officials trace cyclosporiasis outbreak lettuce to Mexico
events.why: Taco Bell recalled lettuce from US restaurants after authorities linked a parasite outbreak to a Mexican supplier, prompting new scrutiny of cross-border food safety.
events.background: Cyclosporiasis is a parasitic intestinal illness that causes vomiting, fatigue and diarrhea, and can spread through contaminated produce.
events.drivers: A joint FDA and CDC investigation traced the sole supplier of shredded iceberg lettuce used at affected Taco Bell locations to Mexico, after outbreaks appeared in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio and West Virginia.
events.implications: US media have named Taylor Farms as the company involved, though neither authorities nor Taco Bell have confirmed it.
events.next: Taco Bell said the affected ingredient from its supplier will be pulled indefinitely.

events.title: Apollo prepares up to 20,000 million dollars to finance infrastructure in Mexico
events.why: The firm is in talks to fund infrastructure projects, particularly in energy and transport, pointing to continued foreign capital interest in Mexico despite trade tensions.
events.background: Commercial-bank credit to Mexico's private sector is only about 35.5% of GDP, a gap foreign private-credit funds have been moving to fill, particularly for infrastructure.
events.drivers: Apollo is in private talks to fund infrastructure projects in energy and transport, a move the article says aligns with President Sheinbaum's strategy of attracting private capital for strategic infrastructure.
events.implications: Projects tied to power generation, renewables and transmission modernization have started gaining momentum under the current administration even as some large-scale projects face delays.

events.title: US rejects Mexico's letters protesting migrant deaths in ICE detention
events.why: Washington returned the diplomatic letters, accusing Mexico of interfering in the management of US detention centers and insisting protests go through formal channels, a test of Sheinbaum's approach to Trump.
events.background: Mexico's government had sent formal protest letters, in the US legal style known as cease and desist, to ICE detention centers where Mexican migrants had died in custody.
events.implications: The rejection signals that Washington will not accept Mexico's direct protests to detention facilities as a channel for raising migrant deaths in ICE custody.
events.drivers: The US State Department's Office of Western Hemisphere Affairs, under Michael Kozak, returned the letters, saying Mexico was attempting to direct the actions of US government personnel operating on US sovereign territory.

events.title: US and Mexico prepare third round of USMCA review talks
events.why: A US negotiating team will travel to Mexico City to discuss steel, autos and trade as both countries work toward the joint review of the USMCA.
events.background: The USMCA review process now runs annually after the US declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, with negotiators meeting repeatedly to work through outstanding issues before deadlines.
events.implications: USTR representative Jamieson Greer credited Economy Secretary Marcelo Ebrard's government with cooperation, suggesting the bilateral track is advancing separately from the broader treaty review.
events.next: The delegations meet July 21 to 23 in Mexico City to advance talks on these sectors.
events.drivers: USTR is sending its negotiating team to Mexico City for a third bilateral round from July 21 to 23, covering steel and aluminum, autos, economic security, labor, agriculture and electronic payment services.

events.title: CPKC advances international arbitration against Mexican government over tax dispute
events.why: The railway operator, which holds concessions for the Pacific North and Northwest networks, is pursuing arbitration in a dispute with Mexican tax authorities.
events.background: CPKC holds concessions over Mexico's Pacific North and Northwest rail networks, corridors the government also wants to use for passenger service.
events.implications: The case tests how tax disputes with foreign concessionaires over strategic rail routes get resolved outside Mexican courts.
events.next: Argentine lawyer Mónica Pinto was named arbitrator on July 3 and will decide the case.
events.drivers: The dispute stems from a 5,000 million peso tax credit the SAT assessed in 2022 over 2014 liabilities, which CPKC has contested through legal appeals before taking the case to international arbitration.

events.title: Mexican regulator may limit America Movil and AT&T in spectrum auction
events.why: The CNA said Telcel and AT&T already hold 33.5% and 26.21% of spectrum for industrial and smart radiocommunication services, prompting possible restrictions on their participation in an upcoming auction.
events.background: Mexico's antitrust regulator, the CNA, oversees competition in telecommunications spectrum, a resource companies need to build private industrial radio networks.
events.implications: Limiting the two dominant carriers could open more of the auctioned spectrum, currently 100 MHz in the 3.5 GHz band, to other bidders.
events.next: The CRT has put the auction's draft rules out for public consultation.
events.drivers: A CRT table shows Telcel holds 33.5% and AT&T 26.21% of spectrum for industrial and smart radiocommunication services nationally, leaving 40.28% still available, prompting Scotiabank to flag likely restrictions on the two firms' access.

events.title: Companies plan electric truck corridor from Mexico City to Nuevo Laredo by 2030
events.why: Bimbo, Femsa, Heineken and other firms are backing charging infrastructure for heavy electric trucks along a key trade corridor to the US border, a step toward decarbonizing freight transport.
events.background: Freight along the corridor between Mexico City and the Nuevo Laredo border crossing is central to US-Mexico trade movement.
events.drivers: The Asociación Nacional de Transporte Privado is leading a joint strategy with companies including Bimbo, Femsa, Heineken, Marva Transportes and Oxxo to map needed investment and infrastructure.
events.implications: Success depends on securing reliable charging infrastructure and grid capacity along the route, the same site-specific power constraints that affect other industrial projects.
events.next: The corridor is targeted for completion by 2030.

events.title: Cotemar plans 1,100 hires as it restarts paused oil projects
events.why: The oilfield services company is resuming onshore and offshore hydrocarbon construction and maintenance work, a signal of renewed activity in Mexico's energy sector.
events.background: Pemex-linked oilfield services support both onshore and offshore hydrocarbon construction and maintenance work in Mexico's energy sector.
events.drivers: The company is restarting previously paused projects, driving plans to add 1,100 specialized jobs for the new work.
events.implications: The hiring signals renewed activity moving down the supply chain to oilfield service providers, though jobs are tied to specific projects rather than permanent roles.

events.title: US health officials link Taco Bell lettuce from Mexico to cyclospora outbreak
events.why: The FDA traced the parasitic outbreak to a single lettuce supplier without naming it publicly, raising food-safety questions for Mexican produce exports to the US.
events.background: US health regulators trace foodborne outbreaks to specific suppliers through investigations that can lead to recalls, a process that affects imported produce like lettuce grown in Mexico.
events.drivers: The CDC and FDA identified a single lettuce supplier behind a cyclospora outbreak tied to Taco Bell locations in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio and West Virginia, though they did not name the company.
events.implications: Taylor Farms is withdrawing its distribution of the implicated lettuce and California-based Taylor Fresh Foods says none of its branded salads are linked to the outbreak.
events.next: Taylor Farms is preparing to recall ingredients tied to the outbreak.

events.title: Ricardo Salinas Pliego sues Sklarov's sons to recover over $50 million
events.why: Salinas Pliego is seeking to recover part of the more than $400 million he says businessman Vladimir Sklarov defrauded him of, in a long-running investment dispute.
events.background: Ricardo Salinas Pliego, owner of Grupo Salinas and TV Azteca, has said financier Vladimir Sklarov, former head of Astor Asset Management, defrauded him of more than $400 million.
events.drivers: Salinas Pliego's lawyers filed suits in federal courts in Illinois and New York against Sklarov's four children and two associates, alleging they knowingly received funds from the fraud.
events.implications: The suits seek to recover about $50 million, alleging the money paid for luxury cars, high-end goods, precious metals and condominiums.

events.title: Audio leaks deepen feud between Baja California governor Marina del Pilar Ávila and former governor Jaime Bonilla
events.why: Ávila says the recordings, tied to a meeting with people she believed were US officials, were leaked by Bonilla, and the fallout threatens the ruling coalition's alliance with the PT in the state.
events.background: Marina del Pilar Ávila and Jaime Bonilla, both former Baja California governors from the ruling coalition, have a long-running political rivalry in the state.
events.drivers: Ávila accuses Bonilla of setting a trap to record and leak calls in which she appears to offer Washington security information in exchange for immunity; Bonilla denies any involvement.
events.implications: The leaked recordings have drawn accusations that Ávila committed treason and threaten the ruling coalition's alliance with the PT in the state.

events.title: USTR delegation to visit Mexico as US pushes to cut trade deficit
events.why: Jamieson Greer says the US wants to shrink its trade deficit with Mexico as USTR sends technical negotiators ahead of the USMCA review, signaling Washington will use the review to press for structural changes in trade flows.
events.background: USMCA's annual review process, triggered after the US declined a 16-year extension, gives Washington repeated openings to press for changes to trade rules.
events.drivers: USTR chief Jamieson Greer said he has a presidential mandate to control the US trade deficit with Mexico using tariffs, quotas or other mechanisms, and to tighten rules of origin in manufacturing sectors.
events.implications: Tighter rules of origin and deficit-control mechanisms could limit growth of Mexican exports to the US market even as Washington says it still prefers importing from Mexico over parts of Asia.

events.title: Rocha Moya case and organized crime complicate USMCA outlook
events.why: The decision to hold annual reviews instead of extending the deal for 16 years is read as political pressure from the Trump administration tied to corruption and cartel cases implicating Mexican officials.
events.background: Mexico's USMCA review shifted from a proposed 16-year extension to mandatory annual reviews after the first joint review in July 2026.
events.drivers: Security analyst David Saucedo argues the shift to annual reviews reflects political pressure from the Trump administration over the case of former Sinaloa governor Rubén Rocha Moya and the lack of Mexican action against him.
events.implications: The bilateral relationship is described as passing through a complex moment shaped by multiple factors beyond trade, including security perceptions of the Mexican government's actions against organized crime.

events.title: Auto industry says sectoral tariffs block rules-of-origin talks with US
events.why: The head of Mexico's auto industry association says automakers paying a 19% tariff to Washington cannot negotiate rules of origin while sectoral tariffs remain in place, tying the USMCA review directly to the sector's cost structure.
events.background: Mexico's auto industry depends on USMCA rules of origin to export vehicles tariff-free to the United States, its dominant market.
events.next: Garza says negotiations are not simple but expects some resolution within the year.
events.implications: The 19% tariff freezes talks on the rules that determine whether Mexican-made vehicles keep tariff-free access, leaving automakers exposed to added costs without a path to renegotiate terms.

events.title: New witness in fuel-theft probe says smuggling network sought help from Ojeda Durán
events.why: A witness in the huachicol fiscal investigation alleges the smuggling ring sought assistance involving a contraband ship from Ojeda Durán, the former navy secretary under López Obrador, deepening a case that has already implicated senior officials.
events.background: The huachicol fiscal case centers on a fuel-smuggling network that grew inside Mexico's Navy secretariat and has already implicated senior military and customs officials.
events.drivers: A new witness said ring leader Miguel Ángel Solano Ruiz sought help from former navy secretary Rafael Ojeda Durán to clear a second contraband fuel ship, the Torn Agnes, through customs in Guaymas.
events.implications: The testimony adds to evidence raising questions about how much Ojeda Durán knew of the conspiracy among military, customs and business figures.
events.next: Defense lawyers for the Farías Laguna family have unsuccessfully requested that Ojeda Durán testify in the case.

events.title: Small gas station owners to meet SAT, Sener and Pemex over diesel price cap
events.why: Independent gas station operators want an explanation of how authorities set the 27-peso ceiling on diesel prices, a policy that affects their margins and could signal broader tension over fuel price controls.
events.background: Mexico has capped diesel prices at 27 pesos, a policy independent gas station owners say squeezes their margins, following earlier disputes over fuel smuggling and tanker truck shortages.
events.implications: Owners remain unconvinced the government has resolved underlying issues like insecurity and fuel truck shortages, despite official claims that these problems are settled.
events.next: The meeting with authorities is set for next Tuesday in Guadalajara.
events.drivers: Around 550 station representatives are pushing authorities including Hacienda, Sener, Pemex, SAT, CNE, ASEA and Profeco to explain how the cap was set, after an earlier Tlaxcala meeting produced no progress.

events.title: Mexican stock exchange expects fewer debt issuances after Pemex-driven surge
events.why: Long-term debt issuance grew more than 80% in the first half of the year, a jump analysts call unusual and tied largely to Pemex's return to bond markets, with volumes expected to normalize.
events.background: The Bolsa Mexicana de Valores channels corporate debt issuance, and Pemex returned to the local bond market in February after a pause of more than six years.
events.drivers: Pemex led first-half issuance with a 31,500 million peso placement, accounting for a large share of the 192,866 million pesos in long-term debt issued by 32 companies, which analysts call an atypical, one-time-driven jump.
events.implications: Analysts expect the pace of debt issuance to slow in the second half of the year once Pemex's large placements are excluded from the comparison.

events.title: Auto sector shift hits Grupo México's rail business
events.why: Grupo México Transportes will lose about 1.5 million dollars a year in revenue after the Tacoma pickup's production changes, showing how shifting auto supply chains ripple into rail freight.
events.background: Rail freight for the auto sector, including vehicles and parts, makes up 72% of Mexico's rail export cargo, according to Mexico's rail and public transport agency ATTRAPI.
events.drivers: Toyota's decision to move Tacoma pickup production from Baja California to Texas ends the weekly rail movement of 30 chassis platforms from Metalsa's Guanajuato plant to Mexicali for assembly in Tecate.
events.implications: Grupo México Transportes will lose about 1.5 million dollars a year in freight revenue, illustrating how shifts in auto manufacturing strategy are reaching rail operators' income.

events.title: Private consumption in Mexico heads toward 15th month of expansion
events.why: Household spending data point to stronger performance in the second quarter, a signal economists watch closely for reading the strength of domestic demand and growth.
events.background: Household spending has been one of the two main pillars of Mexico's weak overall growth, alongside manufacturing exports, as INEGI's private consumption indicator tracks monthly trends.
events.drivers: INEGI's early consumption indicator points to annual growth of 2.8% in May and 2.6% in June, with analysts noting consumption has offset softer performance elsewhere in the economy.
events.implications: Continued consumption growth would extend a 15-month expansion streak even as other components show less momentum.

events.title: Sheinbaum says USMCA review enters key phase, seeks deal before September
events.why: Her comments set a rough timeline for resolving the review, a process now complicated by US demands for tariffs and quotas on Mexican exports.
events.background: USMCA's first joint review in July 2026 saw the US decline a 16-year extension, triggering annual reviews under Article 34.7; Mexico depends on the US for about 80% of its exports.
events.drivers: Sheinbaum said a USTR delegation will visit Mexico the following week, with the government aiming to make the 2026 review the decisive one so future reviews focus only on compliance rather than renegotiation.
events.implications: Mexico's strategy is to lock in clear rules now to avoid recurring uncertainty in future annual reviews.
events.next: The USTR delegation visits Mexico the following week, with the government seeking agreements before September.

events.title: GAFI warns organized crime increasingly launders money through cryptocurrencies
events.why: The financial crime watchdog says gaps in crypto regulation are being exploited by criminal groups to move large sums, pointing to a regulatory gap Mexico and other countries need to close.
events.background: GAFI (the Financial Action Task Force) is the Paris-based intergovernmental body that sets global anti-money-laundering standards, which countries including Mexico are expected to implement.
events.drivers: GAFI says criminal groups have increased use of stablecoins over the past 12 months and some have created their own digital currencies to prevent authorities from freezing or seizing illicit funds.
events.implications: Regulatory gaps in crypto oversight are letting organized crime move large sums of illicit money across borders.

events.title: US tightens visa rules for foreign students, researchers and journalists
events.why: The new limits cap most academic and press visas at four years, part of a broader tightening of entry rules that affects more than two million foreign students and professionals, including Mexicans.
events.background: The Trump administration has pursued repeated restrictions on foreign entrants to the US beyond undocumented immigration, affecting exchange visitors, students and journalists working for foreign outlets, a group that includes Mexicans among more than two million affected.
events.implications: Student visas will be capped at four years, tightening residency conditions for foreign students, researchers and journalists in the US.
events.drivers: The administration says the rule change is needed to end what it calls abuse of these visa categories, announced days after two deaths involving ICE agents.

events.title: Geely brings technology transfer program to Tec de Monterrey
events.why: The Chinese automaker will back research projects, vehicle loans and specialized training in smart mobility as part of its push to build a stronger presence in Mexico.
events.background: Geely, a Chinese automaker that entered Mexico in late 2023, is expanding beyond vehicle sales as Chinese firms deepen their industrial presence in the country.
events.drivers: Geely is investing 4 million pesos with Tec de Monterrey to fund autonomous mobility research, lend EX5 EM-i vehicles, and train engineers and specialists.
events.implications: The partnership signals Geely's effort to build a longer-term technical and educational foothold in Mexico rather than just sell cars.

events.title: AT&T asks regulator to limit Telcel's dominance in 5G spectrum auction
events.why: AT&T is interested in the 3.5 GHz band but has asked the CRT to design auction rules that prevent further spectrum concentration with Telcel, the market leader.
events.background: Mexico's telecom regulator, the CRT, is preparing a 5G spectrum auction after seven years of failed attempts, with Telcel long dominant in the mobile market.
events.implications: The rules set for this auction will shape how competitive Mexico's 5G market becomes relative to Telcel's current dominance.
events.next: The CRT is preparing the 5G spectrum auction for this year.
events.drivers: AT&T told the CRT it wants access to the 3.5 GHz band but is asking for auction rules combining competition safeguards with technical conditions to prevent further spectrum concentration.

events.title: Teachers' union CNTE loses momentum in return to street protests
events.why: The dissident teachers' movement revives demonstrations over what it says are unmet government commitments, but turnout falls well short of the marches that disrupted Mexico City during the World Cup.
events.background: The CNTE is a dissident teachers' movement that staged large protests during the 2026 World Cup, disrupting Mexico City and pressuring the Sheinbaum government over unmet demands.
events.drivers: Only sections 9, 10, 11 and 60 in Mexico City called this protest, and the absence of Oaxaca's Section 22, the largest and most radical bloc, left turnout far smaller.
events.implications: Without Section 22's participation, the CNTE lacks the numbers that previously forced the government to negotiate under pressure.

events.title: BlackRock says Mexico will keep investment grade despite USMCA review risk
events.why: The asset manager flagged that the annual USMCA review process could bring uncertainty for investors but said a downgrade of Mexico's sovereign rating remains unlikely.
events.background: Under USMCA's Article 34.7, Mexico now faces annual treaty reviews after the US declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, and Mexico holds the last investment-grade rung at Moody's, kept there largely by the cost of supporting Pemex.
events.drivers: BlackRock's José Luis Ortega said Mexico's non-confrontational approach in the reviews is meant to build conditions for growth once trade terms stabilize, even as the annual review cycle continues for years.
events.implications: BlackRock sees the review process as manageable rather than a threat to Mexico's sovereign rating, unlike some investor concerns.
events.next: The two countries face a decade of annual USMCA reviews during which an extension agreement could still be reached.

events.title: Mango raises seed round for construction financing platform in Mexico
events.why: The Mexican financial infrastructure startup, which serves the construction sector, brings its total funding to $6 million.
events.background: Mexico's small firms struggle to get credit through banks, which lend only about 35.5% of GDP to the private sector, leaving gaps that fintech platforms are stepping in to fill, including in construction.
events.drivers: The round was led by Brick & Mortar Ventures with Ironspring Ventures, and Mango plans to expand data infrastructure, launch four new products, and grow headcount by 44% over nine months.
events.implications: Mango's platform, which already works with over 230 distributors and has processed $16M in transactions, aims to build credit histories for construction firms otherwise locked out of financing.
events.next: Mango plans to launch four new products and grow headcount by 44% over the next nine months.

events.title: Nexu secures $143 million credit line from HSBC
events.why: The Mexican fintech, which provides AI-powered automotive financing, will use the funding to expand its lending capacity.
events.background: Auto lending in Mexico is constrained by the same credit gap affecting small firms, pushing fintechs to serve consumers with limited credit histories that traditional banks avoid.
events.drivers: HSBC extended the $143M credit line to let Nexu expand lending, with the company already working through more than 1,000 dealerships covering about half of Mexico's auto market.
events.implications: The financing will let Nexu fund over 10,000 additional vehicles, widening credit access for buyers banks typically turn away.

events.title: Aviva raises $18 million Series A led by Valor Capital
events.why: The Mexican fintech's round, backed by Valor Capital Group, adds to a wave of venture funding into the country's financial technology sector.
events.background: More than half of Mexico's workforce operates informally, largely excluded from bank credit, which is where fintechs like Aviva aim to serve a market of over 50 million underbanked adults.
events.drivers: The round was led by Valor Capital Group with BID Labs, Caravela Capital, Endeavor and existing investors, funding expansion of Aviva's kiosk-based, AI-video loan model across Mexico.
events.implications: Aviva, active in 23 of Mexico's 32 states with over 300,000 completed applications, is scaling a small-loan model built specifically for the informal economy.
events.next: Aviva will use the funds to expand across Mexico and launch new financial products.

events.title: Finsus updates its banking file as it plans expansion across Latin America
events.why: The Sofipo's CEO confirmed plans to bring operations to Central America as it pursues a full banking license, part of its broader growth strategy.
